description: A leaf in wind or a lip before tears may 'tiripe': move in small, rapid, repeated beats that are often involuntary. Twitching and fluttering use the same verb when that motion is the point. 'roke' (move) is broader; cold or fear can be named as the cause.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper front teeth for unvoiced dental 't' and release into high-front unrounded 'i'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue at the alveolar ridge, then return to stressed high-front 'i'. Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release into middle-height front unrounded 'e'.
sound_symbolism: Dental closure, a quick tongue tap, and lip closure make three small changes of contact across the word. The two high 'i' vowels keep its middle narrow. The result feels clipped and flickering, close to the motion of a quiver.
usage_notes: 'tiripe' is intransitive. A cause may take 'lue' (ABL), as in quivering from cold, and an adjective before the verb can describe the motion's pace or intensity. As a noun, it is a quiver or tremor.

semantic_domains.nature: small repeated movement in leaves, flame, wings, or an animal's body
semantic_domains.physical: rapid involuntary or lightly controlled motion of a body or part
